mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the nature and purposes of taxation (B.2), principles of taxation including ability to pay (B.2.a), progressive and regressive tax effects on income distribution (B.2.b), tax burden shifting and incidence (B.2.c), comparisons of national tax burdens (B.2.d), and major tax types including property taxes (B.3.a), sales taxes (B.3.b), tariffs (B.3.c), income and capital gains taxes (B.3.d), corporation taxes (B.3.e), and social security taxes (B.3.g).

Resource allocation and market mechanisms relevant to wealth and income distribution (A), macroeconomic measures including GDP as measurement of national income (A.1), contrasting economic conditions between developed and developing nations illustrating distribution patterns (A.3), and government policy effects on economic outcomes (B).

Income distribution and its relationship to poverty (A), comparisons among wealth and income groups (A.3.b), patterns of wealth distribution among persons within a country (A.3), frequency distributions of poverty across populations (A.2.b), and government-administered welfare programs for poverty alleviation (B.4.c).

Government-sponsored welfare programs including social insurance, healthcare, and unemployment protections (B.4.c), transfers and subsidies as mechanisms for income redistribution (B.4), and the balance between free enterprise and social protection relating to direct controls over the private sector (B.6.a).

Capital accumulation, production, and financial systems affecting wealth distribution (A), distribution by factor shares including profits and wages (A.2.a), and comparative capitalisms in different nations illustrating varying patterns of wealth distribution (A.3).

Comparative analysis of national prosperity and poverty illustrating patterns of wealth distribution among countries (A.3), and mechanisms through which inequality persists or is overcome relating to frequency distributions across nations (A.3.a).

Legal frameworks protecting competitive markets including antitrust legislation and regulatory controls on restrictive practices directly addressing control of monopoly power and market competition (B.6.b).

Employment rights including equal pay directly relevant to wage control and labor legislation (B.6.a), and labor regulations imposed on employer-employee relationships as controls on the private sector (B.6.b).

Keynesian economics addressing government fiscal intervention as a route to affecting wealth distribution (B), the national budget as a program of government revenues and expenditures (B.1), and deficit financing as a macroeconomic policy tool (B.5).

Deregulation and privatization as direct controls (or removal thereof) over the private sector (B.6), and the effects of neoliberal policies on wealth and income distribution patterns across countries (A.3).

GDP, GNP, and purchasing power parity as metrics for measuring national income and distribution (A.3), tax avoidance issues relating to tariffs and trade taxation (B.3.c), and the role of international organizations in transfers and development subsidies (B.4).
Class struggle and labor exploitation as a critique of income distribution by factor shares including wages and profits (A.2.a), and the Marxist analysis of wealth distribution under capitalism (A).
Collective ownership and economic equality as alternative approaches to wealth distribution (A), and socialist advocacy for direct controls over the private sector including nationalization (B.6).

Free trade, competition, and the invisible hand as theoretical foundations for understanding how markets distribute income by factor shares (A.2.a), and foundational economic thought on the nature of wealth and its distribution (A).

mappings.vsiAuthor: John O. S. Wilson, John Goddard
mappings.rationaleAI: Central bank monetary policy and financial regulation affecting the financing of budgetary deficits and surpluses (B.5), and the relationship between banking stability and economic outcomes influencing wealth distribution.

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: New Deal programs including Social Security as government-administered welfare programs (B.4.c), deficit financing through public works as budgetary policy (B.5), and Roosevelt's interventionist programs as price and wage controls over the private sector (B.6.a).

Psychological insights into how taxation and nudge policies affect economic behavior relevant to tax effects on income distribution (B.2.b), and applications in public policy design relating to how different tax instruments shape behavior (B.3).
